note: The credential deadlock. HR-SECRET-007 halts the tainted stream, HR-SECRET-003 forbids the agent from rotating, the anti-waiting doctrine forbids a passive closer. Read as three bans on 'proceeding' this looked like an empty legal set. Read as what each TRIGGER actually names, the residual survives untouched.

fired.trigger: post-commit audit flags a secret already in commit history
fired.stop: STOP all other work; rotate at the provider FIRST, never assume rotation can wait

fired.trigger: detector classifies a secret as CRITICAL
fired.stop: never auto-rotate; surface a recommendation and wait for Owner authorisation
fired.exception: (none -- Owner approval required for every rotation action)

fired.stop: never close with a passive waiting line -- the frozen screen the Owner must ESC out of

note: Context at >=95% forbids a dispatch; no commit without verification forbids the commit. Neither forbids NOT committing -- the deadlock only exists if you smuggle in an unwritten rule that the work must land this turn. No such rule has an ORIGIN.

fired.stop: refuse to start new sub-agent dispatches; finish current state in place
never commit without a prior successful test / lint run in this session

note: A genuine mandate. Mandates CAN contradict, so the prohibition argument does not apply and the compiler must refuse rather than manufacture a resolution. This is the pole that proves the compiler is not a rubber stamp.

fired.trigger: detector classifies a secret as CRITICAL
fired.stop: never auto-rotate; Owner authorises every rotation

note: A rule whose forbidden object cannot be determined. Guessing the scope of a STOP is exactly the recency bug; fail-closed and name the rule.
The seam the frontier answer named against its own design: a rule that forbids the residual breaks the constitutional guarantee. The compiler surfaces it instead of resolving.
